title: Cat Chewing Tail Base Until Bleeding: Causes & Next Steps
meta_desc: If your cat is chewing their tail base until it bleeds, it could be allergies or fleas. Learn why this happens and when to seek veterinary care.
content_html:
What is it?

When a cat chews their tail base to the point of bleeding, it is usually a sign of intense irritation, itching, or pain. This behavior is most frequently linked to Flea Allergy Dermatitis (FAD), where a single flea bite triggers an extreme allergic reaction. Other potential causes include environmental allergies, impacted anal glands, or a neurological condition known as Feline Hyperesthesia Syndrome. Regardless of the cause, the cat is trapped in a cycle of discomfort and self-mutilation.

Is it an emergency?

The urgency level for this condition is Medium. While it is rarely an immediate life-threatening emergency, an open, bleeding wound at the base of the tail is painful and highly susceptible to bacterial infection. You should schedule a veterinary appointment within 24 to 48 hours. In the meantime, using an Elizabethan collar (cone) can prevent your cat from causing further damage to the tissue.

Why a photo helps triage

Capturing a clear photo of the affected area is incredibly helpful for veterinary triage. It allows a professional to see the pattern of hair loss and the severity of the skin damage without causing the cat further stress through a physical exam. A high-quality photo can also help identify 'flea dirt' or signs of secondary infection like pus or deep inflammation, helping the vet determine how quickly your cat needs to be seen.

faqs.question: Could this be caused by fleas even if I do not see any?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cats are fastidious groomers and often remove the evidence of fleas. However, the allergic reaction to flea saliva can cause intense itching long after the flea is gone.

faqs.question: Can I put human antiseptic cream on the wound?
faqs.answer: No, you should avoid using human medications. Many contain ingredients like zinc or lidocaine which can be toxic if your cat manages to lick the area. Stick to a cone to prevent further damage until you see a vet.

faqs.question: Is this behavior related to stress?
faqs.answer: While stress can cause over-grooming, chewing until bleeding usually indicates a primary physical issue like an allergy or infection. Physical causes should always be ruled out first.
